edges.target: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

edges.description: Central-ray loss in the hand produces a median cleft or split-hand malformation.
edges.source: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

edges.description: Central-ray loss in the foot produces a split-foot malformation.
edges.source: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

edges.description: Loss or hypoplasia of central digits with median clefting corresponds to ectrodactyly.
edges.source: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

edges.description: Dysregulated FGF-BMP-MSX signaling alters interdigital apoptosis, producing syndactyly of remaining digits.
edges.source: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

edges.description: SHFM-associated disruption of digit identity and patterning can include supernumerary digits in some subtypes.
edges.source: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

edges.description: Central-ray absence reduces the number of digits, producing oligodactyly.
edges.source: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

nodes.description: The unifying mechanism across SHFM loci is failure to maintain normal apical ectodermal ridge (AER) function during limb development. The AER is the epithelial signaling center at the distal tip of the limb bud that sustains outgrowth of the underlying mesenchyme. SHFM-associated mutations converge on dysregulation of FGF8 in the central portion of the AER, failure of AER stratification, and disruption of reciprocal Wnt-BMP-FGF signaling between the AER and progress-zone mesenchyme. The central-ray defect reflects loss of this signaling specifically in the median AER.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic regulatory pathways of split-hand/foot malformation.
evidence.snippet: Split-hand/foot malformation (SHFM) is caused by mutations in TP63, DLX5, DLX6, FGF8, FGFR1, WNT10B, and BHLHA9.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic regulatory pathways of split-hand/foot malformation.
evidence.snippet: The mutations in SHFM-related genes lead to dysregulation of Fgf8 in the central portion of the apical ectodermal ridge (AER) and subsequently lead to misexpression of a number of downstream target genes, failure of stratification of the AER, and thus SHFM.

evidence.reference_title: A complex structural variant near SOX3 causes X-linked split-hand/foot malformation.
evidence.snippet: SHFM is caused by failure to maintain normal apical ectodermal ridge function during limb development.

nodes.description: Loss of central-AER FGF8 dysregulates the reciprocal Wnt-BMP-FGF signaling loop. Disruption of canonical and non-canonical Wnt signaling, together with altered FGF-BMP-MSX signaling, drives the phenotype: median clefts with absent or hypoplastic central rays, syndactyly of the remaining digits (from dysregulated apoptotic cell death), and loss of digit identity (from misexpression of HOX genes and an abnormal SHH gradient or loss of GLI3A/GLI3R balance).
nodes.id: Disrupted Wnt-BMP-FGF Signaling and Central Ray Loss

evidence.reference_title: Genetic regulatory pathways of split-hand/foot malformation.
evidence.snippet: Disruption of canonical and non-canonical Wnt signaling is involved in the pathogenesis of SHFM.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic regulatory pathways of split-hand/foot malformation.
evidence.snippet: Syndactyly of the remaining digits is most likely the effects of dysregulation of Fgf-Bmp-Msx signaling on apoptotic cell death.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic regulatory pathways of split-hand/foot malformation.
evidence.snippet: Loss of digit identity in SHFM is hypothesized to be the effects of misexpression of HOX genes, abnormal SHH gradient, or the loss of balance between GLI3A and GLI3R.

nodes.description: Absence of one or more central digits with median clefting, the defining limb defect of SHFM.

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis Imperfecta and Split Foot Malformation due to 7q21.2q21.3 Deletion Including COL1A2, DLX5/6 Genes: Review of the Literature.
evidence.snippet: We report a Turkish girl with ectrodactyly, joint laxity, multiple bone fractures, blue sclera, early teeth decay, mild learning disability, and depression.

nodes.description: Reduced digit number reflects hypoplasia or absence of central rays in split hand-foot malformation.
Supernumerary digits, classically described in SHFM6 (WNT10B) though present in only a minority of patients.

evidence.reference_title: WNT10B variants in split hand/foot malformation: Report of three novel families and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Polydactyly, which was classically described in SHFM6, was only present in 27% of patients.

nodes.description: Median cleft of the foot with absent or hypoplastic central rays; in many SHFM forms the feet are more severely affected than the hands.

evidence.reference_title: WNT10B variants in split hand/foot malformation: Report of three novel families and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: The feet are more severely affected than the hands and there is a frequent asymmetry without obvious side-bias.

nodes.description: Median cleft of the hand with absent or hypoplastic central rays, producing a lobster-claw appearance.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic regulatory pathways of split-hand/foot malformation.
evidence.snippet: This implies that in normal situations these SHFM-associated genes share an underlying regulatory pathway that is involved in the development of the central parts of the hands and feet.

nodes.description: Fusion of remaining digits, frequently affecting the third and fourth fingers, attributed to dysregulated apoptotic cell death in the interdigital mesenchyme.

evidence.reference_title: WNT10B variants in split hand/foot malformation: Report of three novel families and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Syndactyly of third-fourth fingers was a frequent finding (62%).
